method_atom: A computational scheme for many-body systems with quenched (frozen) disorder in their interaction strengths: estimate the density of states over energy via entropic Monte Carlo sampling (a flat-histogram / Wang-Landau algorithm run in dominant subspaces), then extract critical exponents by finite-size scaling and use them to classify the universality of a collective transition. This lets one test whether frozen randomness in couplings is irrelevant (same exponents plus logarithmic corrections) or converts a discontinuous transition into a continuous one with a distinct set of exponents, and to introduce local order-densities that reveal disorder-induced microsegregation.
object_atom: A two-dimensional square-lattice ferromagnetic Blume-Capel model (interacting three-state units under a crystal-field anisotropy), studied in both pure and random-bond forms across its first-order and second-order regimes, transitioning between ferromagnetic and paramagnetic phases.
brief_reason: The disorder-averaged density-of-states sampling plus finite-size-scaling exponent extraction and universality classification is the transferable machinery, while the specific lattice spin model transitioning between ferromagnetic and paramagnetic phases is the physics-specific object.
